---
title: "P(robabilities), E[xpectations], and the Truth"
title-slide-attributes:
    data-background-image: images/probability.png
    data-background-size: fill
    data-background-opacity: "0.5"
author:
    - name: Roemer J. Janse
      orcid: 0000-0003-0059-872X
      email: R.J.Janse-5@umcutrecht.nl
      affiliations:
        - name: UMC Utrecht
format: 
    revealjs:
        theme: simple
---

```{r set-up}
#| message: false
#| warning: false
#| include: false

# Load packages
pacman::p_load("conflicted",     # Package conflicts
               "dplyr",          # Data wrangling
               "magrittr",       # Better pipelines
               "knitr",          # Nice tables
               "tidyr",          # Data pivoting
               "ggplot2",        # Data visualisation
               "gganimate",      # Data animation
               "pvaluefunctions",# P functions
               "healthmotionR"   # Surgery data
)

# Resolve conflicts
conflicts_prefer(dplyr::filter)   # Between stats and dplyr

```

# Rationale

**Understanding** >>> Doing

::: {.fragment}
Want to know which test to use: [Wikistatistiek AMC Toetskeuzetabel](https://wikistatistiek.amc.nl/KEUZE_TOETS#Schema_welke_toets_bij_welke_vergelijking)
:::

::: {.fragment}
Want to know how SPSS works: [Youtube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZpwZS3XnEZA)
:::

# Disclaimer {background-image=images/statistician.jpg background-opacity="0.3"}

::: {.fragment}
**I am not a statistician nor philosophist**
:::

::: {.fragment}
We want to apply probability so:
:::

::: {.fragment}
We focus on developing an intuitive feeling for probability, foregoing unnecessary detail for application.
:::

## Agenda
- Conflating Probability and Reality
- Probability: Our Fake Best Friend
- Expectations Instead of Probabilities
- Maximising experience (or scientific FOMO)
- How Can We Make the Truth Probable?

# Conflating Probability and Reality

## What is Probability?
::: {.fragment}
A reflection of our uncertainty:
:::

::: {.incremental}
- *It will probably be...*
- *The likelihood that...*
- *There is a risk of...*
- *We have a chance to...*
- *I expect that...*
:::

## What is Probability?
Specifically, the chance that some phenomenon $Y$ occurs.\
*E.g. the chance of perforation during PCI*\
\

::: {.fragment}
Expressed as either a percentage (bounded by 0% and 100%), or a proportion (bounded by 0 and 1).
:::

## What is Probability?
We denote the probability of $Y$ as $P(Y)$\
*(and sometimes as $Pr(Y)$ or $\mathbb{P}(Y)$)*\
\

::: {.fragment}
If the probability is conditional on some factor $X$, we use $|$:
$P(Y|X)$, which reads as the probability of Y given X
:::
\

::: {.fragment}
For instance the probability of perforation during PCI given that the patient is >70 years old.\
$P(Y|X>70)$
:::

## Is Probability?
::::{.r-fit-text}
Is there still probability beyond our own (ignorance-driven) uncertainty?\
What we believe influences the way we think about probability.\
\

::: {.fragment}
**Deterministic worldview**\
The future *can* be perfectly predicted if we understood all moving parts.
:::
\

::: {.fragment}
**Stochastic worldview**\
The future *cannot* be perfectly predicted, even if we understood all moving parts.\
Truly (non-negligible) random processes exist 
:::
::::

## Dealing With a Complex World
Regardless of our worldview, our technology is too primitive to make deterministic statements about:

|
-|---
our world|e.g. the prevalence of heart failure
our actions|e.g. the effect of a heart transplantation on survival
the future|e.g. who is most likely to get a complication during surgery

## Dealing With a Complex World
::::{.r-fit-text}
Our solution?\
\

:::{.fragment}
Take a guess of the probability
:::

:::{.fragment}
*Based on experience*
:::
\

:::{.fragment}
```{r tabular data}
# Set random seed
set.seed(1)

# Get 5 random indeces
vec_indeces <- sort(sample.int(nrow(surgerydat_df), 5))

# Print surgery table
kable(surgerydat_df[vec_indeces,])
```
:::
::::

# Expectations Instead of Probabilities
## Continuous Expectations
Probabilities apply the occurrence of phenomena.\
\
In other words: *'Did an event happen or not?'*\
\

However, we may also want to say something about continuous values:\

:::{.incremental}
- What is the average height?
- What is the expected future quality of life?
- How does this procedure improve functioning as measured on some scale?
:::

## Continuous Expectations
Probabilities do not apply to continuous values.\
\
Instead, we talk about the expected value of some continuous value $Y$:\
\
$E(Y)$ (or $\mathbb{E}(Y)$)\
\
This is generally the same as the mean.

## Continuous Expectations
Sometimes (often), people will want to categorise continuous measures\
\
```{r categorisation}
#| fig-height: 3

# Set seed
set.seed(1)

# Fake data
dat_plot <- tibble(group = factor(c("Underweight", "Within range", "Overweight"),
                                  levels = c("Underweight", "Within range", "Overweight")),
                   xmin = c(15, 18, 25),
                   xmax = c(18, 25, 30),
                   ymin = 0,
                   ymax = 1)

# Draw plot
ggplot(dat_plot,
       aes(xmin = xmin,
           xmax = xmax,
           ymin = ymin,
           ymax = ymax,
           fill = group)) +
    # Geometries
    geom_rect(colour = "black") +
    # Scales
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("#648FFF", "#DC267F", "#FFB000")) +
    scale_x_continuous(name = expression(paste("BMI (kg/", m^2, ")")),
                       breaks = c(15, 18, 25, 30)) +
    # Aesthetics
    theme(plot.background = element_blank(),
          panel.background = element_blank(),
          panel.grid = element_blank(),
          axis.line.x = element_line(),
          axis.text.y = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
          axis.title.y = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "bottom",
          legend.title = element_blank())

```

# Maximising experience \ (or scientific FOMO)
## Gaining Experience (Sampling)
:::{.column}
```{r true proportion sex}
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 6

# Rename data
dat_plot <- surgerydat_df

# Calculate proportion male
prp <- paste0(format(round(proportions(table(dat_plot[["sex"]]))[["male"]], 3) * 100), "%")

# Get absolute count male
cnt <- table(dat_plot[["sex"]])[["male"]]

# Create ground truth plots
ggplot(dat_plot,
       aes(x = 1,
           fill = sex)) +
    # Geometries
    geom_bar(position = position_stack()) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = cnt,
               linetype = "dashed",
               colour = "black") +
    geom_text(aes(x = 1.39,
                  hjust = 1,
                  y = cnt * 1.05,
                  label = prp)) +
    # Scales
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(name = "Proportion",
                       breaks = seq(0, nrow(dat_plot), length.out = 5),
                       labels = paste0(seq(0, 100, length.out = 5), "%"),
                       expand = c(0,0)) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("#FFB000", "#DC267F")) +
    # Labels
    ggtitle("Total population",
            subtitle = "n = 32 529") +
    # Transformations
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0.6, 1.4)) +
    # Aesthetics
    theme(panel.border = element_rect(fill = "transparent",
                                      colour = "black",
                                      linewidth = 1),
          plot.title = element_text(size = 12,
                                    hjust = 0.5),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10,
                                       hjust = 0.5,
                                       face = "italic"),
          legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "bottom",
          axis.text.x = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
          axis.title.x = element_blank())

```
:::

::::{.column}
:::{.fragment}
```{r sample proportion sex}
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 6

# Set seed for random processes
set.seed(1)

# Rename data
dat_plot <- surgerydat_df

# Create sampling groups
dat_plot %<>% 
    # Create groups
    mutate(# Add ID
           id = row_number(),
           # Add group
           group = cut(id, 32529, labels = FALSE)) %>%
    # Arrange per group
    arrange(group) %>%
    # Group per group
    group_by(group) %>%
    # Calculate nr. of males in each group and size of group
    mutate(males = sum(if_else(sex == "male", 1, 0)),
           n = n(),) %>%
    # Keep one row per group
    slice(1L) %>%
    # Remove grouping structure
    ungroup() %>%
    # Keep only relevant variables
    select(group, males, n) %>%
    # Calculate cumulative proportions
    mutate(cum_males = cumsum(males),
           cum_n = cumsum(n),
           cum_prp_males = cum_males / cum_n,
           cum_prp_females = 1 - cum_prp_males) %>%
    # Pivot to long data
    pivot_longer(c(cum_prp_males, cum_prp_females),
                 names_to = "sex",
                 values_to = "prp") %>%
    # Change labels
    mutate(sex = if_else(sex == "cum_prp_males", "male", "female"))
    
# Calculate proportion male
prp_line <- proportions(table(surgerydat_df[["sex"]]))[["male"]]

# Create ground truth plots
ggplot(dat_plot[1:4000, ],
       aes(x = 1,
           y = prp,
           fill = sex,
           label = paste0("n = ", cum_n))) +
    # Geometries
    geom_bar(position = "stack",
             stat = "identity") +
    geom_hline(yintercept = prp_line,
               linetype = "dashed",
               colour = "black") +
    geom_label(aes(x = 1,
                   y = 0.25),
               fill = "white") +
    # Scales
    scale_x_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(name = "Proportion",
                       limits = c(0, 1),
                       breaks = seq(0, 1, length.out = 5),
                       labels = paste0(seq(0, 100, length.out = 5), "%"),
                       expand = c(0,0)) +
    scale_fill_manual(values = c("#FFB000", "#DC267F")) +
    # Labels
    ggtitle("Sample population",
            "Random sample of n = 2000") +
    # Transformations
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(0.6, 1.4)) +
    # Aesthetics
    theme(panel.border = element_rect(fill = "transparent",
                                      colour = "black",
                                      linewidth = 1),
          plot.title = element_text(size = 12,
                                    hjust = 0.5),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10,
                                       hjust = 0.5,
                                       face = "italic"),
          legend.title = element_blank(),
          legend.position = "bottom",
          axis.text.x = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks.x = element_blank(),
          axis.title.x = element_blank()) +
    # Animation
    transition_states(group,
                      transition_length = 5,
                      state_length = 3)

```
:::
::::

## Gaining Experience (Sampling)
:::{.column}
```{r true mean bmi}
#| fig-width: 6
#| fig-height: 6

# Rename data
dat_plot <- surgerydat_df

# Get mean BMI
bmi <- mean(dat_plot[["BMI"]])

# Create ground truth plots
ggplot(dat_plot,
       aes(x = BMI)) +
    # Geometries
    geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5,
                   colour = "black",
                   fill = "#FFB000",
                   alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = bmi,
               linetype = "dashed") +
    # Scales
    scale_x_continuous(name = expression(paste("BMI (kg/", m^2, ")")),
                       breaks = c(15, 18, 25, 30)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    # Labels
    ggtitle("Total population",
            subtitle = "n = 32 529") +
    # Transformations
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(10, 46)) +
    # Aesthetics
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 12,
                                    hjust = 0.5),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10,
                                       hjust = 0.5,
                                       face = "italic"),
          panel.grid = element_blank(),
          panel.background = element_blank(),
          axis.text.y = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
          axis.title.y = element_blank())

```
:::

::::{.column}
:::{.fragment}
```{r sample mean bmi}
#| fig-height: 6
#| fig-width: 6

# Rename data
dat_plot <- surgerydat_df

# Create sampling groups
dat_plot %<>% 
    # Create groups
    mutate(# Add ID
           id = row_number(),
           # Add group
           group = cut(id, 32529 / 10, labels = FALSE)) 

# Create empty plot for extending data
dat_plot_extend <- tibble()

# Create plotting data
for(i in 1:200){
    dat_plot_extend %<>% 
        # Add new data
        bind_rows(
            # All groups so far
            filter(dat_plot, group <= i) %>%
                # Set group indicator
                mutate(group = i)
        )
}

# Calculate values
dat_plot_extend %<>%
    # Arrange per group
    arrange(group) %>%
    # Group per group
    group_by(group) %>%
    # Calculate size
    mutate(n = n()) %>%
    # Remove grouping structure
    ungroup() 

# Create ground truth plots
ggplot(dat_plot_extend,
       aes(x = BMI,
           label = paste0("n = ", n))) +
    # Geometries
    geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.5,
                   colour = "black",
                   fill = "#FFB000",
                   alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_vline(xintercept = bmi,
               linetype = "dashed") +
    geom_label(aes(x = 12,
                   y = 10),
               fill = "white") +
    # Scales
    scale_x_continuous(name = "BMI (kg/m2)",
                       breaks = c(15, 18, 25, 30)) +
    scale_y_continuous(expand = c(0, 0)) +
    # Labels
    ggtitle("Sample population",
            subtitle = "Random sample of n = 2000") +
    # Transformations
    coord_cartesian(xlim = c(10, 46)) +
    # Aesthetics
    theme(plot.title = element_text(size = 12,
                                    hjust = 0.5),
          plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 10,
                                       hjust = 0.5,
                                       face = "italic"),
          panel.grid = element_blank(),
          panel.background = element_blank(),
          axis.text.y = element_blank(),
          axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
          axis.title.y = element_blank()) +
    # Animation
    transition_states(group,
                      transition_length = 5,
                      state_length = 3)

```
:::
::::

## Gaining Experience (Sampling)
Importantly:\
\

:::{.fragment}
Which (theoretical) population are we sampling?\
:::
\

:::{.fragment}
Try sampling for yourself:\
[UU Sampling App](https://utrecht-university.shinyapps.io/sampling_app/)
:::

## Minimally Required Experience
**Three approaches:**

*Calculate sample size*

:::{.incremental}
- Only works beforehand
- Prior data must be available
:::

*Convenience sample size*

:::{.incremental}
- Sample might still not be sufficient
:::

*Simulate sample size*

:::{.incremental}
- Prior data must be available
:::


## Minimally Required Experience
:::{.r-fit-text}
**Some well-established calculations**

|Focus|Study type|Outcome type|Calculation|
|--|---|-----|----|
|Causal|Any|Continuous & Dichotomous|[Jansen *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1093/ejendo/lvae129)|
|Diagnostic|Any|Dichotomous|[Buderer *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1553-2712.1996.tb03538.x)|
|Prognostic|Development|Continuous|[Riley *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.m441) & [Whittle *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1186/s41512-026-00228-9)|
|Prognostic|Development|Dichotomous|[Riley *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.m441) & [Riley *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1186/s41512-025-00193-9)|
|Prognostic|Development|Survival|[Riley *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj.m441) & [Riley *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1186/s41512-025-00204-9)|
|Prognostic|Validation|Continuous, Dichotomous & Survival|[Riley *et al.*](https://doi.org/10.1136/bmj-2023-074821)|

etc...
:::

## Population focus
:::{.incremental}
- Experience = observations = (often) individual people
- For the individual, $P(Y)$ will be 1 or 0 and $E(Y)$ may not exist
- The average individual is a myth: [Daniels' *The Average Man*](https://apps.dtic.mil/sti/tr/pdf/AD0010203.pdf)
- Traditional RCTs give us population effects (interesting for policy-makers)
- Modern methods give us individualised effects (interesting for healthcare providers)
- Ecological fallacy: *...when conclusions about individuals are drawn from conclusions about the total population...*
:::

# How Can We Make the Truth Probable?
## Thinking About Truth
:::::{.r-fit-text}
::::{.column}
*Frequentist thinking*

:::{.incremental}
- How (statistical) scientists are trained to think
- There is some true $P(Y)$ or $E(Y)$ in the long run
- Based on our hypothesis of that true value, would we expect these data? *or*:
- $P(data|hypothesis)$
- Requires frequent sampling
:::
::::

::::{.column}
*Bayesian thinking*

:::{.incremental}
- How humans tend to think
- We only have our current experience to inform us
- Based on what we have seen so far (prior data), what will we see next (posterior data)? *or*:
- $P(hypothesis|data)$
- Requires good priors

:::
::::
:::::

## Thinking About Truth
The way of thinking is situation-dependent:

:::{.incremental}
- A fair die: frequentist approach
- An unfair die: Bayesian approach
:::
\
\

:::{.fragment}
Most importantly, reflect on how you think and, on the situation, and choose what is appropriate.
:::

## A Look Inside the Frequentist Toolbox
::::{.r-fit-text}
**P-values**\
A p-value is the probability of your observed difference or larger occurring, given that there is not actually a difference (i.e. probability of type 1 error / false positive).\
\

:::{.fragment}
Often dichotomised (p-value < 0.05), leading to loss of information.\
\
:::

:::{.fragment}
```{r p-function}
#| fig-height: 4
# Calculate mean age difference between groups 
diff <- with(surgerydat_df, mean(age[sex == "male"])) - with(surgerydat_df, mean(age[sex == "female"]))
# 0.03264118 years older for men

# T-test
p <- t.test(age ~ sex, data = surgerydat_df, var.equal = FALSE)[["p.value"]]
# p-value = 0.8823

# Create p-value function
dat_plot <- conf_dist(
  estimate = diff,
  df = c(31403), 
  tstat = c(-0.14807), 
  type = "ttest", 
  plot_type = "p_val", 
  n_values = 1e4, 
  conf_level = 0.95, 
  null_values = 0,
  alternative = "two_sided",
  xlab = "Mean age difference in years for males",
  plot = FALSE
)[["res_frame"]] %>%
    # Drop missing values
    filter(!is.na(values))

# Counternull (value with equal evidence as null)
counternull <- 0.06528236

# Draw plot
ggplot(dat_plot,
       aes(x = values,
           y = p_two)) +
    # Geometries
    geom_line() +
    geom_vline(xintercept = c(0, counternull),
               linetype = "dashed",
               alpha = 0.5) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = p,
               linetype = "dashed",
               alpha = 0.5) +
    # Scales
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = c(seq(-0.8, 0.8, 0.4), round(counternull, 2), round(diff, 2))) +
    scale_y_continuous(breaks = c(seq(0, 1, 0.2), 0.05)) +
    # Labels
    ggtitle("P-function for age difference between males and females") +
    xlab("Mean age difference in years (males compared to females)") +
    ylab("Two-sided p-value") +
    # Aesthetics
    theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45,
                                     hjust = 1,
                                     vjust = 1),
          plot.title = element_text(face = "bold",
                                    hjust = 0.5))

```
:::
::::

## A Look Inside the Frequentist Toolbox
::::{.smaller}
**Confidence intervals**\
The confidence interval contains the true value in 95% of all confidence intervals for the corresponding hypothesis.\
\

:::{.fragment}
This means that the probability of any specific confidence interval containing the truth is either 0% or 100%!\
:::
\

:::{.fragment}
The confidence interval relates directly to the p-value. If a 95% confidence interval contains the null-value, the p-value will be > 0.05). Resultingly, confidence intervals are also often interpreted as a dichotomy.
:::
::::

## A Look Inside the Frequentist Toolbox
**Bootstrapping**\
Re-performing your analyses on different samples drawn from your sample.\
\

:::{.fragment}
Assumption 1: our data contains (for the majority) all types of observations that may occur.\
Assumption 2: those observations may occur in different frequencies in different samples.\
:::
\

:::{.fragment}
We draw random individuals from our data (with replacement!) to create a new sample.
:::

## A Look Inside the Frequentist Toolbox
**Bootstrapping**\
![](images/bootstrap.png){height=500}

## Closing Remarks
To work with probability, we need to understand it.\
\

:::{.fragment}
There are many philosophical ideas that describe how we think about statistics *and*\
:::
\

:::{.fragment}
There are many statistical ideas that describe how statistics (and its measures, such as probability) can work.\
:::
\

:::{.fragment}
A scientist working with statistics is responsible to have at least a working knowledge of these ideas, if they truly want to understand their work.
:::

## The End
Contact me: [r.j.janse-5@umcutrecht.nl](mailto:r.j.janse-5@umcutrecht.nl)\
\

More about me: [rjjanse.github.io](https://rjjanse.github.io)\
\

These slides: [rjjanse.github.io/talks/theoretical_probability](https://rjjanse.github.io/talks/theoretical_probability)
